items.inspection_target: finite distance circles are conics from one pencil; the absolute is the infinite-radius member
items.representation: conic pencil generated by M.T B M and A
items.validation: each plotted circle is obtained from the circle-through-point matrix formula

items.inspection_target: all finite circles around the same center touch the absolute at the polar intersections
items.representation: outside center, polar line, exceptional points, and tangent residuals
items.validation: circle and absolute tangent lines are parallel at both contact points

items.inspection_target: large Euclidean circles flatten to a finite line plus the line at infinity
items.representation: Euclidean centers m=(a,b,c) with c tending to zero
items.validation: normalized conic matrix converges to the source-span limit matrix

items.inspection_target: circle, horocycle, and hypercycle differ by center position and real contact pattern
items.representation: classification panel and definition dependency graph
items.validation: contact counts and graph nodes are recorded in visual checks

items.inspection_target: Galilean distance uses x-coordinate difference, while cycle theorems live on vertical parabolas
items.representation: vertical-line distance circles, parabolic cycles, and theorem probes on y=x^2
items.validation: slope-difference and secant-product identities are checked exactly and numerically

items.concept: Galilean cycle as a deformation limit
items.inspection_target: hyperbola-like, parabola, and ellipse-like cycles meet at k=0 as the ideal points coalesce
items.representation: Plotly slider through conics with ideal quadratic part x^2 + k y^2
items.validation: every slider conic passes through the same three finite points

library_routing.matplotlib: durable 2D conic diagrams and theorem probes
library_routing.sympy: exact Galilean angle and secant-product identities
